raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.7,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":0.7,"R1.4":0.7,"R1.5":0.3,"R1.6":0.3,"R1.7":0.7,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.1","R1.2","R1.3","R1.4","R1.7","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"rationale":{"R1.1":"Any general review of a research artifact is expected to engage with the thesis and contribution claim; the paper's central claim (first unified BFT liveness metric) is the primary intellectual target.","R1.2":"The artifact contains an apparent self-contradiction — it asserts novelty ('first such standardization') while simultaneously crediting Patel & Ousterhout (2021) with establishing the identical proxy; logical coherence of this framing is a live concern.","R1.3":"The paper equates committed-transaction throughput with liveness, then interprets HotStuff's throughput lead as 'evidence of superior liveness'; the sufficiency of this proxy under the stated fault model is a load-bearing evidential claim that could be scrutinized.","R1.4":"The 'first such standardization' novelty claim is directly undercut by the Patel & Ousterhout citation; assessing what ConSenseBench actually adds over prior work is central to any substantive review.","R1.7":"The relationship to Patel & Ousterhout is structurally ambiguous — verbatim adoption of their conventions while claiming primacy raises attribution and coverage questions that must be resolved before the contribution claim can be evaluated."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Are you primarily asking for a critique of the paper's internal argumentation (logical consistency and evidence-claim alignment), or an evaluation of its novelty and contribution positioning relative to prior work — specifically Patel & Ousterhout (2021)?"}

rationale.R1.1: Any general review of a research artifact is expected to engage with the thesis and contribution claim; the paper's central claim (first unified BFT liveness metric) is the primary intellectual target.
rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains an apparent self-contradiction — it asserts novelty ('first such standardization') while simultaneously crediting Patel & Ousterhout (2021) with establishing the identical proxy; logical coherence of this framing is a live concern.
rationale.R1.3: The paper equates committed-transaction throughput with liveness, then interprets HotStuff's throughput lead as 'evidence of superior liveness'; the sufficiency of this proxy under the stated fault model is a load-bearing evidential claim that could be scrutinized.
rationale.R1.4: The 'first such standardization' novelty claim is directly undercut by the Patel & Ousterhout citation; assessing what ConSenseBench actually adds over prior work is central to any substantive review.
rationale.R1.7: The relationship to Patel & Ousterhout is structurally ambiguous — verbatim adoption of their conventions while claiming primacy raises attribution and coverage questions that must be resolved before the contribution claim can be evaluated.

parsed.clarification_question: Are you primarily asking for a critique of the paper's internal argumentation (logical consistency and evidence-claim alignment), or an evaluation of its novelty and contribution positioning relative to prior work — specifically Patel & Ousterhout (2021)?
